35f62abd-9802-808b-a9b9-fb8ce9e931bd:
# Adaptive Scheduler

This is my project **Adaptive Scheduler**.

## Main Workflow

```
Voice → transcribe → keyword extraction used to create the reminder → create reminder → fire reminder → notification
```

So, basically the whole workflow goes down to:

> **Reminder creation, Reminder Firing**

## Now lets get technical!

### The Prompt

First most important thing in this project, since I am using **Groq APIs**, is the prompt. Let's see the flow of it.

Normally all the code used to just stay in one large file, `agent.py`. But I know that **architecture is very important** when building an Agent. So I turned that one file into components and connected them all one by one:

- `router.py` — figures out what the user actually wants (create/update/delete/query a reminder), just plain keyword matching, no LLM call needed for this part
- `context.py` — grabs the user's timezone before the agent even starts thinking
- `tools.py` — the actual functions that touch the database (get reminders, search, find free gaps, etc.)
- `prompts.py` — yep, prompts are a separate file now too
- `orchestrator.py` — ties everything above together, this is the actual loop

And the prompt part specifically — instead of one giant prompt trying to handle *everything* (creating, editing, deleting, answering questions), I split it into **4 separate prompts**, one per workflow, and only the relevant one gets loaded depending on what `router.py` decides. So the *create* prompt only knows about creating, the *query* prompt only knows about answering — nobody's carrying instructions they don't need.

### The Tools Layer

Every one of those JSON actions the LLM picks maps to an actual Python function — the model **never touches the database directly**, it only ever says *"I want to call this tool with these arguments"* and a separate piece of code does the real work. So there's a clean line: the LLM decides *what* to do, plain Python does *how*.

### Duration rules

> - **Instant stuff** (drink water) → `0`
> - **Fixed-duration stuff** (exam, movie) → agent asks once if u didn't mention it
> - **Self-paced stuff** (reading, studying) → agent asks how long *u* plan to spend, not how long the task "takes" (since that doesn't even make sense for something like reading a book)
> - **Open-ended decision stuff** (like picking a problem statement for a hackathon) → no duration at all, since there's no sensible number for that
>
> Agent asks only *once* and moves on with a default if u don't answer — it doesn't keep pestering u.

### Memory — how the conversation stays in sync

The backend doesn't actually hold onto the conversation between messages — every time I send something, the *entire* conversation history gets sent along with it, and the server rebuilds context from scratch each time. Sounds inefficient but it's actually the opposite — the server stays completely stateless, which means it can handle way more people at once without needing to remember who's talking to it or juggle sessions.

### Notifications & Cron Jobs

The application needs to check *every single minute* whether any reminder needs to fire — pre-alerts, the actual on-time notification, missed re-fires, and follow-ups. So I was thinking about how to do this and then I got to know about **cron jobs**!

I built one endpoint on the backend (`/cron/check-reminders`) that does all four of those checks whenever it's pinged. The only piece left was: *what pings it, every minute, forever, for free?*

There's quite a few options for this:
- **UptimeRobot**
- **cron-job.org**
- **Render's own Cron Jobs** feature

I ended up going with **cron-job.org**, and there's an actual reason for it, not just random pick — UptimeRobot's free tier only checks every **5 minutes**, which isn't fast enough for something that needs to fire *on time*. Render does have native Cron Jobs, but even the smallest one has a **$1/month minimum charge** — not exactly free. `cron-job.org`, on the other hand, lets you run jobs **every single minute, completely free**, no catch.

## Converse with the agent about your past & upcoming schedule

**This one i am most proud of.**

U can literally ask the agent things like *"do i have a gap today"* and it'll actually check your reminders between **7AM–6PM** and tell u exactly where u are free — not just guess.

Or ask something like *"did i attend the wedding last month"* and it'll search through your done/missed reminders and answer u — even if u don't phrase it exactly like the reminder title was.
38d62abd-9802-8015-9d77-d3168b421580:
# Maze2Race 🏁

So, this is a simple and fun project which so many people have built and i too tried to build my own version!

## About the project

Used three different algorithms and demonstrated their abilities on a maze board. here's what makes my version interesting and special:

- **three boards to try**: an open field where u can draw ur own walls, a proper generated maze with loops (so its not just one single path from start to end), and an unreachable board to test that the algorithms correctly say "no path found" instead of breaking
- **draggable start and end points**, so u can move them anywhere on the board and re-run
- **slo-mo mode**, where u can literally watch the algorithm explore node by node, with a speed control, plus pause and stop buttons
- **compare mode**, where all three algorithms run on the same board side by side, so u can see the difference in nodes covered and path cost at a glance

So, on the app we can see the path taken by the algorithms in slo-mo and u can control the slo-mo speed and can pause or stop it.

Then another one is compare algorithms! u can see the comparison between the three algorithms in terms of nodes covered and path cost! All three boards with the marked path and extra nodes covered will be seen side by side by clicking the arrow!

And more algorithms i wanna try in future!

## Controls

- pick a **board** (open field / maze / unreachable) and an **algorithm** (BFS / Dijkstra / A*) from the dropdowns up top
- hit **Draw Walls** to toggle draw mode, then click or drag on the grid to add walls, click again to remove them
- u can also just grab the red (start) or blue (end) dot and drag it anywhere on the board
- **Clear Walls** wipes all the walls u drew, **Reset** puts everything back to the board's default
- hit **Run** to solve it instantly, or turn on **Slo-Mo** first to watch it explore node by node, with a speed control, pause and stop
- switch to **compare mode** to see all three algorithms run side by side on the same board, then use the arrows to flip through each one's path, nodes covered, and path cost

## What I learned with this project

so, i know a little about the algorithms that i used in this project.

**BFS**, i know very well. it just visits all its neighbours before exploring further. it doesn't have any intelligence, it just keeps exploring layer by layer until it reaches the destination.

**Dijkstra**, it keeps track of the real cost from the start to every node using a min heap, and always picks the node with the lowest known cost so far, which is the root of the min heap! and yeah, when u remove the root, the min heap undergoes heapify to adjust itself so the smallest comes back to the root, that's a relief! so it keeps expanding the cheapest node until it reaches the destination. it's guaranteed to find the shortest path, but it has no idea where the destination actually is, so it ends up exploring in every direction pretty equally!

**A***, lets see. in all these algorithms we can see they either just go by instinct (bfs) or by real cost so far (dijkstra). but A* goes further in intelligence, it uses the advantage and the assumption of the fact that it knows the destination coordinates. but i was like, how would it know! but yeah, in most of the cases we do know the destination, like, we as humans, when solving mazes know the destination. A* takes advantage of that. so the algorithm calculates a heuristic, a guess of the distance from the current node to the destination node, and combines that guess with the real cost so far to decide which node to explore next. so it doesn't blindly trust the guess, it balances "cost so far" + "estimated cost remaining", which is why it usually explores way fewer nodes than dijkstra while still finding the same shortest path!

i did learn about these when i was learning Data Structures, and A* i learned in my course AI. But to see them in action like this is very cool! watching them work live is so cool! and yeah, i am going to implement more algorithms and check how they work! there's more, maybe i can find something that finds the path effectively without knowing the destination beforehand!
